answer:
In Chapter 7 of *Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I ("On the territory proper to each nation"), Charles Comte presents the territory of a nation as the highest form of property by extent and importance—after things held in common by humanity—enclosing and enabling all subordinate properties (e.g., those of provinces, communes, families, and individuals).[^1] He observes this as a universal, ancient fact: every habitable part of the globe has long been occupied by nations or tribes (civilized or barbarous), formed from families grouping into hordes, each claiming exclusive property over a delimited space providing its means of existence.[^2][^3]

Comte emphasizes that such national territories arise naturally from human associations and necessities, not civil laws or governments distributing land.[^4] Diplomatic treaties legitimizing conquests by violence are not morally binding unless they align with natural justice; a nation may break them when strong enough, prioritizing duties to its own members.[^5] Violations provoke fierce wars—especially among savages, for whom territory is synonymous with survival—often resolved by treaties or sales recognizing limits.[^6]

The guarantee of a nation's territory lies not in international governance but in defensive necessity, natural barriers (seas, mountains), allied interests, and overwhelming obstacles to invasion—what Comte calls the "law of nations."[^7]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I, ch. 7, [p-2cfb18](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-2cfb18).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I, ch. 7, [p-b9fe30](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-b9fe30).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I, ch. 7, [p-523da2](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-523da2).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I, ch. 7, [p-982e4a](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-982e4a).

[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I, ch. 7, [p-dc0e30](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-dc0e30) and [p-9de914](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-9de914).

[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I, ch. 7, [p-0178a5](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-0178a5).

[^7]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I, ch. 7, [p-982e4a](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-982e4a).
